item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to the inability to casually observe SCP-19231, a stationary camera has placed on a nearby asteroid of similar orbit, designated SCP-1923-Watcher.

This has a small grammer issue. "a stationary camera has placed" should either be "a stationary camera has been placed" or "a stationary camera was placed".

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Author has released the image under CC BY-SA 3.0.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The reason why it couldn't be source was because, you know, I drew it.

For those who are curious and reading this, no attempt was made by anyone on staff to ask me about the source of the image in question.

vOv

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is to be assumed as non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Expungements in containment procedures is a huge no no for me, considering the datestamp on the earliest complaint about it i just cant forgive that -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one. It did leave a lot to my imagination, but also has enough going on that I'm not left completely in the dark about this thing and what it may or may not be doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me the potential for a hook was the redacted last line, but being redacted took the point away.
No-vote here.

item.title: Reading is a skill
item.content:encoded: As I said previously, the picture is of the existing asteroid, and couldn't do much editing without it looking like a spaceship.

item.title: Nice SCP
item.content:encoded: The last part is what hooked me on this one, great job and interesting concept. Plus one right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The second half of your third-to-last paragraph and your second to last paragraph really opened my eyes about this SCP. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a cool article but, and no offense, but the picture looks like you cut something out of frickin' Terraria. I know it's unreasonable to expect Avatar shit but…. it took me out a little. Upvoted anyway coz it's neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note: Asteroid also contains three (3) miniscule volcanoes, two (2) of which are active, and one (1) rose. In addition, the tree has been confirmed to be a Baobab, not a palm tree.

Upvoted by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At the risk of being arrogant, the redactions and expungements are not pointless, nor were they placed there frivolously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mostly restating what others have before me: I like it, but all the pointless redactions and expungements really kind of ruin it. Upvoting for quality, but please clear some of those up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe this is who was trying to communicate with SCP-831.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The creep factor isn't that it's something weird and quasi-unexplainable but far far away. The creep factor is that it's something weird and quasi-unexplainable that's beaming a transmission directly at Earth that we can't yet translate. After it got a bit of clean up was done, this one definitely got my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I feel it is too far away for the Foundation to be involved with anything but distant observation. The Lunar base was a real stretch of resources and it was only done because of the teleporter. Use the Hubble to observe it instead of missions to plant cameras. I also have issues with the changing orbit concept. Additionally, I feel that it is so far away that it loses much of the creepy factor. If it was a weird tree growing on an abandoned satellite orbiting Earth I would worry about it more. The Moon is close to us. The Asteroid Belt is a long, long, long way away. Just my novice opinion, mind you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Out-of-universe answer: I'm not that great at photoshop, and removing the craters suddenly made the thing look like a spaceship instead of an asteroid.

In-universe answer: the canopy is always 95m above the surface. So a crater on the surface is reflected in a crater-looking shape in the canopy.

Trivia about your question: I originally had a line in the piece that explained that the Foundation once assumed that SCP-1923 was just a covering of moss or lichen, precisely because it covered the asteroid so precisely. I took it out because it seemed like immaterial information, but I'll consider putting it back in as a footnote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it, but there's an issue. If the canopy completely obscures the surface of the asteroid, why can we see craters in the photo? I think I preferred it when it was just the one tree sticking out and a variety of grass and flowers providing the color.

Anyway, +1 for being creepy and far away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for appealing to my love of weird things what are in space.

item.title: About the expunges
item.content:encoded:
The expunges exist because there's something that I had a hell of a time explaining, because the situation is by-design extremely difficult to figure out. See, the 'tree' in question is trying to communicate with Earth in some form or another, but the Foundation can't figure it how it's doing it, what method is being used, much less what it's saying. I don't have the scientific background to explain 'it's a signal, but it's not a signal we understand'. I'll be trying to clean it up, in addition to some other things.

Analogy time: the 'tree' is sending a image file of a waveform of a man whispering ancient Egyptian. In reverse. Oh, and it's sent to a computer screen that can only output 0s and 1s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. Don't like the last few sentences's [EXPUNGED]s. Poorly done, don't imply anything really.

Upvoted anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me going right up to all of the redaction at the end…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I thought the picture was an anglerfish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. The fact that quite a lot is unknown simply because it is hard to get to is very plausible.

Some suggestions

As pro-mole said, fauna is animals, flora is plant like

Redacting the actual ID of the asteroids involved makes perfect sense but does make it hard to follow . For example the sentence 'All signals originating from ███ ██████ are to be recorded and analyzed.' is ambiguous, unless that is your intention given addendum 2. Have you considered giving the different asteroid sub SCP designations e.g. SCP-1923-1,- 2, -3?

While you have indicated all conclusions are tenuous given the quality of photos, if the foundation can get a camera close enough to determine colours and relatively modest scale objects (leaves on the shedding 'tree', movement of the newer object) they should be collecting enough data to perform some basic spectroscopy. That would let someone say with more confidence if the growth is reflecting light in the same way as Earth chlorophyll does. That doesn't mean though that the actual results wouldn't be classified, redacted or informative but the foundation should be able to determine slightly more than they have. If the infection's metabolism is in anyway analogous to the chlorophyll cycle then being able to reach that height in the limited amounts of light (good old inverse square law) and free carbon where they are, with enough excess energy to power movement is pretty damn terrifying and the foundation would be right be be concerned about it targeting earth.

The fact that the foundation have before and after shots to estimate the height of the growth suggests they are monitoring lots and lots of asteroids on an ongoing basis which is itself pretty cool.

I am happy to up vote this and am interested in seeing how this will develop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just skimmed and… doesn't "fauna-like" mean it's similar to an animal, not a plant?

And I don't know if I like the way the footnotes are written. Well, footnote 1, at least. Sounds like an author's comment on a book, not a footnote on a scientific article.

But this does look intriguing. I'll really get to read this later on.

item.title: Well, um...
item.content:encoded: That's actually a failure of my meager photoshop skills. It's actually a tree that I was trying to draw as if the cameras could barely pick it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I can see that the protruding tree is pretty clearly a satellite, and the "trunk" is its trail, but this is very nice. Quite unique, and fairly ominous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It is interesting without being "oh my god, it's going to kill us all!".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in chat it's very interesting and I'm curious to see if anything comes of it.

Have an upvote.

item.title: Some detail about this SCP
item.content:encoded:
I always do one of these for an SCP, so I figured I'd do one for this one.

This SCP was inspired by a dream I had while I was sick a few weeks ago. Vaguely like the Genesis Planet video in Wrath Of Khan, but instead of fire or anything, it was nature. A tree sprung up, and then a wave of greenery covered the planet. Really strange dream, and it stuck with me enough to hammer something out as a rough SCP skeleton. Cut to a week later, when I'm blabbing about the dream to a friend of mine. He shrugged and said "So you had a dream about Dyson Trees?" I looked it up, and was shocked, since I've never seen anything like it before. I decided to keep working on the thing, because I liked the mystery of the idea. What is it? Who made it? Where did it come from? Why is it trying to do something with Earth? I chose the number 1923, since that Dyson guy was born in 1923.

The asteroid I used was 951 Gaspra, which coincidentally is in the 'Flora' grouping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's interesting, but it doesn't have a hook. It's just kinda there. So no vote for now, but I'm anticipating an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or go in and fix them…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would you kindly elaborate which grammar mistakes exist?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way to many grammar mistakes, and I don't usually care about grammar mistakes, but whooooee. Also the picture needs to be placed or none at all.

The scp itself is ehh, and the writing isn't that great.
